chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Welcome to the 11th lecture of 6.006, our first lecture

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: on weighted shortest paths.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Until now, we've only been talking about graphs

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: where we measure distance in terms

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: of the number of edges in a path.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Today, we're going to generalize that notion.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: But I just want to go over what we've talked

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: about in the last two lectures.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: In the last two lectures, we've talked

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: about two algorithms, breadth-first search

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: and depth-first search, to solve a range of problems.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Here's some of the problems that we've been solving.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Single source shortest paths, where distances

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: are measured in number of edges in a path.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And we use BFS to solve this problem,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: starting from a single source, usually a vertex s that we call.

chunks.title: Lec

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: We solve that in order v plus e.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: That's what we called linear time for a graph.

chunks.text: For the special case of single source reachability,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: here we had to return a shortest path

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: distance for every vertex.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And there was, at most, e things reachable from a vertex.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So this is the bound we got.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: But in the special case for single source reachability,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: when our output only has to list the vertices that

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: are reachable from me, the number of things

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: reachable in basically a spanning

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: tree of the connected component of my source

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: can almost be of order e.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: I don't really care.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So I can get this in order e.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: But that's kind of a little optimization.

chunks.text: The next thing we did was we talked

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: about connected components.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: one connected component, then I can

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: look at any other vertex I haven't seen

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: and explore the next one.

chunks.text: And so that, actually, with a little analysis,

chunks.title: Lec

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: All right, in this lecture and in, actually,

chunks.text: It's OK to be 0 as well.

chunks.title: Lec

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: we're going to be given a weight function that

chunks.title: Lec

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: you might have this be real numbers.

chunks.text: But in this class, we're going to deal with integers.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So each edge, if you have an edge,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: we're going to say this is the weight of this edge e from e.

chunks.text: So why do we care about adding weights to our graph?

chunks.text: Well, this comes up a lot in many applications.

chunks.text: For example, distances in a road network.

chunks.text: to Central Square, we might think of that as one road.

chunks.text: Maybe you've got each road is a connection

chunks.text: So we might want to associate a larger distance or a weight
